info.set_name: Comprehensive Respiratory Set 1: Pulmonary Circulation and V/Q (Short Answer)

q1.prompt: What is the normal mean pressure in the pulmonary artery compared to the mean pressure in the aorta?,
correctOptionIds: Pulmonary artery mean pressure is approximately 15 mmHg, whereas aortic mean pressure is 100 mmHg.,

q2.prompt: Why is pulmonary vascular resistance (PVR) significantly lower than systemic vascular resistance (SVR)?
correctOptionIds: Because the pulmonary vessels are more compliant and the pressure head required to drive the same cardiac output is much lower.,

q3.prompt: In a person with healthy lungs, by what factor is pulmonary vascular resistance lower than systemic vascular resistance at rest?
correctOptionIds: Approximately one-sixth.

q4.prompt: How do the pulmonary blood vessels respond to an increase in pulmonary arterial pressure or cardiac output to prevent a massive rise in pressure?,
correctOptionIds: Through the recruitment of underperfused vessels and the distention of patent vessels.,

q5.prompt: What is the primary factor that regulates pulmonary blood flow by causing local vasoconstriction?,,
correctOptionIds: Alveolar P_(O2)(hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction).,,

q6.prompt: According to the gravitational effect on hydrostatic pressure, by how much does the arterial pressure increase for every centimetre below the heart's level?
correctOptionIds: 0.77 mmHg.

q7.prompt: Where in the lungs is blood flow lowest when a person is standing upright?,
correctOptionIds: The apex (top) of the lungs.,

q8.prompt: Describe the pressure relationship that characterizes Zone 1 of the lung, and state what can cause this zone to become prominent.,,
correctOptionIds: PA>Pa>Pv; it can occur if arterial pressure is decreased (hemorrhage) or alveolar pressure is increased (positive pressure ventilation).,,

q9.prompt: In which lung zone is blood flow driven by the difference between arterial pressure and venous pressure (Pa>Pv>PA )?,
correctOptionIds: Zone 3.,

q10.prompt: What is the "physiologic shunt," and what are its two primary anatomical sources?,,
correctOptionIds: It is the small fraction of cardiac output (~2%) that bypasses oxygenation; the sources are bronchial blood flow and coronary blood flow via thebesian veins.,,

q11.prompt: What is the characteristic effect of a right-to-left shunt on systemic arterial P_(O2) ?
correctOptionIds: Hypoxemia (decreased systemic arterial P_(O2)).
The ratio of alveolar ventilation to pulmonary blood flow.,

q13.prompt: What is the normal average value for theV˙/Q˙  ratio for the entire lung?,,
correctOptionIds: Approximately 0.8.,,

q14.prompt: How does theV˙/Q˙  ratio change from the base to the apex of the upright lung?,
correctOptionIds: It increases from the base (~0.6) to the apex (~3.0).,

q15.prompt: Which specific region of the upright lung (apex or base) has the highest Pa_(O2)and the lowest Pa_(CO2) ?,
correctOptionIds: The apex.,

q16.prompt: What is theV˙/Q˙  ratio in a lung region where the airway is completely obstructed?,
correctOptionIds: Zero.,

q17.prompt: What gas composition (P_(O2)and P_(CO2)) does pulmonary capillary blood approach when theV˙/Q˙  ratio is zero?
correctOptionIds: It approaches mixed venous blood levels (P_(O2)=40 mmHg and P_(CO2)=46 mmHg).

q18.prompt: What is theV˙/Q˙  ratio in a lung region where blood flow is completely blocked by an embolism?,
correctOptionIds: Infinite.,

q19.prompt: What happens to the alveolar P_(O2)and P_(CO2)in a region of dead space whereV˙/Q˙  is infinite?
correctOptionIds: They approach the values of humidified inspired air (P_(O2)=150 mmHg and P_(CO2)=0 mmHg).

q20.prompt: Why does a right-to-left shunt result in an increased A-a gradient?,
correctOptionIds: Because shunted (venous) blood bypasses oxygenation and dilutes the P_(O2)of the arterialized blood.,

q21.prompt: What happens to the distribution ofV˙/Q˙  ratios throughout the lung during strenuous exercise?,
correctOptionIds: TheV˙/Q˙ratio becomes more uniform or "even" throughout the lung.,

q22.prompt: Why does breathing 100% O2have a limited effect on raising arterial P_(O2)  in a patient with a large right-to-left shunt?,,
correctOptionIds: Because the shunted blood never reaches the ventilated alveoli to be oxygenated, regardless of the alveolar P_(O2).,,

q23.prompt: What is the effect of increased lung volume on the resistance of alveolar capillaries versus extra-alveolar vessels?
correctOptionIds: Increased lung volume crushes alveolar vessels (increasing resistance) but pulls open extra-alveolar vessels (decreasing resistance).

q24.prompt: At which specific lung volume is total pulmonary vascular resistance at its lowest point?
correctOptionIds: Functional Residual Capacity (FRC).

q25.prompt: Name three local vasoactive substances (besides oxygen) that act as pulmonary vasodilators.,
correctOptionIds: Prostacyclin (Prostaglandin I2), nitric oxide, and bradykinin.,

q26.prompt: What is the effect of Thromboxane A2 on pulmonary vascular resistance?,
correctOptionIds: It is a powerful local vasoconstrictor of both pulmonary arterioles and veins.,

q27.prompt: By what mechanical mechanism does the body compensate for the increased pulmonary resistance caused by chronic global hypoxia at high altitude?,
correctOptionIds: Right ventricular hypertrophy.,
